title: VTE in Pregnancy
oneLiner: Acute venous thromboembolism occurring during pregnancy or postpartum; leading cause of direct maternal death in the UK.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive PE:** Sudden hypoxia, hypotension, right heart strain (distended neck veins, loud P2) → **Bedside echo showing right ventricular dilation** – do not wait for CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) if haemodynamically unstable.
- **Amniotic fluid embolism:** Sudden cardiorespiratory collapse *during labour or immediately after delivery* + disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) – **differentiate by timing (labour/postpartum) and presence of DIC**; very rare, but mimics massive PE.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage from cerebral venous sinus thrombosis:** Severe headache, seizures, focal neurology in a pregnant/postpartum woman – **CT venogram or MRI venogram** is critical; do not give heparin if this is the diagnosis.
sections.Presentation:
- **DVT:** Unilateral leg pain, swelling, warmth, red/erythema – usually left leg (left iliac vein compression by gravid uterus). Homan's sign (unreliable).
- **PE:** Dyspnoea (most common), pleuritic chest pain, tachypnoea, tachycardia, cough, haemoptysis. Syncope or hypotension in massive PE.
- **Upper limb DVT:** rare – arm swelling, pain, collateral veins (consider central line-related).
- **Postpartum:** Days 1–42 after delivery; peak risk in first week.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **DVT:**
  - **Superficial thrombophlebitis** – tender, palpable cord, no distal swelling.
  - **Cellulitis** – erythema with fever, lymphangitis, no oedema.
  - **Muscle haematoma/r/o** – bruising, trauma history, unilateral haematocrit drop.
  - **Baker's cyst** – usually popliteal fossa, not extending to calf.
  - **Physiological oedema** – bilateral, mild, resolves overnight.
- **PE:**
  - **Pneumothorax** – sudden unilateral chest pain, hyperresonance, tracheal shift if tension.
  - **Pneumonia** – fever, purulent sputum, focal crackles.
  - **Asthma exacerbation** – wheeze, known history, reversible.
  - **Musculoskeletal chest pain** – reproducible tenderness.
  - **Anxiety/hyperventilation** – normal saturations, normal blood gases, triggers.
- **Splitter:** **Unilateral leg swelling + positive compression ultrasound (CUS) → DVT; bilateral leg swelling + proteinuria + hypertension → pre-eclampsia, not DVT.**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** ECG (tachycardia, S1Q3T3 if massive PE; often normal), pulse oximetry (SaO2 <94% suspicious), D-dimer **NOT useful** – physiologically elevated in pregnancy (use clinical probability first).
- **Bloods:** FBC (platelets – baseline before anticoagulation), clotting screen (PT, APTT), renal function (LMWH dose adjustment), LFTs (if anticoagulation history), VBG/ABG (low PaO2, respiratory alkalosis).
- **Imaging:**
  - **For suspected DVT:** **Compression ultrasound (CUS)** of leg veins – first line. If negative but high clinical suspicion, repeat CUS in 1 week or consider whole-leg ultrasound.
  - **For suspected PE:**
    - **Chest X-ray** – done to look for alternate diagnosis (pneumothorax, pneumonia). If abnormal → CTPA. If normal → **V/Q scan** or CTPA.
    - **CTPA** – lower radiation to fetal breast but higher to maternal breast (lifetime risk). Use modern low-dose protocols.
    - **Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan** – higher fetal radiation, lower maternal breast. Acceptable alternative if CXR normal and no contraindications.
    - **Echocardiogram** – emergently if massive PE to assess right heart strain.
  - **Never-miss test:** **Compression ultrasound (for DVT)** or **CTPA/VQ (for PE)** – omitting imaging in a high-probability patient is lethal.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Modified Wells score for PE in pregnancy is not validated** – use **PERC rule** (if all negative, low risk) but in pregnancy with dyspnoea/tachycardia, imaging is usually needed. **For DVT,** clinical diagnosis is unreliable; ultrasound is gold standard.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If massive PE with shock:** Call senior + ICU; consider immediate thrombolysis (e.g. alteplase 0.6 mg/kg IV over 15 min – only after ruling out intracranial haemorrhage and absolute contraindications). Do **not** delay for CTPA if bedside echo shows RV strain and clinical suspicion is high.
  - **Raise legs** for DVT (not for PE – increase preload slowly).
  - **Oxygen** to target SpO2 >94%.
  - **IV access**, baseline bloods.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Diagnostic imaging** (see above).
  - **Anticoagulation:**
    - **Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH)** – first line (enoxaparin or dalteparin). **Do not use unfractionated heparin (UFH) unless massive PE or severe renal impairment.**
    - Dosing: based on booking weight (ideally early pregnancy weight, otherwise current weight). Give weight-adjusted once daily.
    - For acute VTE: treatment dose. Example: enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily or 1 mg/kg twice daily (both acceptable – check local policy). Dalteparin 200 U/kg once daily.
    - **Check platelets weekly for first 2 weeks** (HIT very rare with LMWH but document).
    - **Anti-Xa monitoring** – not routinely required except extremes of body weight (<50 kg or >90 kg) or renal impairment – [check local policy].
  - **Thrombolysis** – only for massive PE with haemodynamic instability; discuss with obstetric medicine + haematology + cardiothoracic.
  - **IVC filter** – rarely indicated; only if contraindication to anticoagulation (e.g. active bleeding, upcoming delivery within 2 weeks with new DVT) – [check local policy].
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue LMWH for remainder of pregnancy and at least 6 weeks postpartum (total minimum 3 months).
  - **Postpartum:** Switch to warfarin (INR 2–3) or continue LMWH – warfarin is safe postpartum, but LMWH preferred in many units if patient prefers no injections or has contraindications.
  - **Plan for delivery:** Convert to UFH (if using LMWH and regional anaesthesia planned) – stop LMWH at least 24h before induction/C-section. [Check local policy for exact timing; often 12–24h.]
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid only if hypotensive; avoid fluid overload in right heart strain.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not applicable once on treatment dose LMWH.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if thrombolysis or surgery planned.
  - **Observation:** 4-hourly vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2) for first 24h; continue daily weight (for LMWH dose recalculation if weight changes >10%).
  - **Mobilisation:** Active mobilisation (no bed rest) – compression stockings (if DVT, after acute swelling reduced).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line – LMWH:** Enoxaparin (Clexane) – **treatment dose**: 1.5 mg/kg once daily SC OR 1 mg/kg twice daily SC. Dalteparin (Fragmin) – 200 U/kg once daily SC. Dosing based on **booking weight** (or current if >20% difference).
- **Alternatives:** UFH – IV infusion (target APTT 1.5–2.5 baseline) or SC twice daily – used if severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min) or near delivery. Fondaparinux – if HIT (rare in pregnancy).
- **Pain control:** Paracetamol (1g QDS PO/IV); avoid NSAIDs (risk of premature ductus closure, oligohydramnios).
- **Key contraindications:** Active bleeding, recent haemorrhagic stroke, severe thrombocytopenia (<50×10⁹/L), epidural/spinal anaesthesia planned within 12–24h of LMWH dose.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** In severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min) – use UFH (dose with APTT monitoring) or reduce LMWH dose – [check local policy]. Hepatic impairment: no specific adjustment but monitor clotting.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily to a woman >100 kg without checking anti-Xa – risk of underdosing in obesity; consider twice-daily regimen or anti-Xa monitoring.** Also: **Not holding LMWH 24h before epidural/spinal** – risk of spinal haematoma.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** **Massive PE** (death), **paradoxical embolism** (arterial occlusion), **heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)** – very rare with LMWH, **bleeding** (minor > major; risk of retroplacental haematoma, postpartum haemorrhage).
- **Chronic:** **Post-thrombotic syndrome** (leg swelling, pain, venous ulcers – up to 50% of DVT patients), **chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension** (CTEPH – post-PE, dyspnoea, right heart failure – rare).
sections.Prognosis: - Maternal mortality from VTE ~1–2% overall; higher with massive PE. Fetal loss rate ~5% in treated VTE. Full recovery likely with anticoagulation; post-thrombotic syndrome common but can be minimised with early compression stockings.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use a clinical probability tool before imaging** (e.g. simplified Geneva or PERC) – but in pregnancy, any clinical suspicion + hypoxia or unilateral leg swelling = imaging.
- **Check booking weight for LMWH dose** – avoid using current weight late in pregnancy if oedema distorts.
- **Prescribe compression stockings** for DVT (after oedema reduced) – reduce post-thrombotic syndrome.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use D-dimer to rule out VTE in pregnancy** – it is elevated physiologically and in many conditions; leads to unnecessary imaging or false negatives.
- **Do not start LMWH without checking baseline FBC (platelets) and coagulation** – to identify pre-existing coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia.
- **Do not continue LMWH through delivery** – must be timed or switched to UFH to allow regional anaesthesia and reduce bleeding risk.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Chest pain, shortness of breath, haemoptysis, leg swelling/worsening pain, bleeding from any site, vaginal bleeding, abrupt severe headache.
- **Follow-up:** Obstetric medicine clinic within 1 week; haematology (if anticoagulation complex); postpartum anticoagulation plan; repeat ultrasound at 6 weeks if DVT to check recanalisation.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Massive PE:** Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) + hypoxia → call ICU/medical emergency team (MET); consider thrombolysis.
- **Signs of bleeding:** Vaginal bleeding > any spotting, or retroplacental haematoma (abdominal pain + fetal distress) → call senior obstetrician; stop LMWH until assessed.
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >2 mmol/L** – escalate to registrar/consultant.
- **GCS drop** or **seizure** – rule out cerebral venous thrombosis; immediate CT head/venogram.

rawMarkdown:
## VTE in Pregnancy
**One-liner:** Acute venous thromboembolism occurring during pregnancy or postpartum; leading cause of direct maternal death in the UK.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive PE:** Sudden hypoxia, hypotension, right heart strain (distended neck veins, loud P2) → **Bedside echo showing right ventricular dilation** – do not wait for CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) if haemodynamically unstable.
- **Amniotic fluid embolism:** Sudden cardiorespiratory collapse *during labour or immediately after delivery* + disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) – **differentiate by timing (labour/postpartum) and presence of DIC**; very rare, but mimics massive PE.
- **Intracranial haemorrhage from cerebral venous sinus thrombosis:** Severe headache, seizures, focal neurology in a pregnant/postpartum woman – **CT venogram or MRI venogram** is critical; do not give heparin if this is the diagnosis.

### Presentation
- **DVT:** Unilateral leg pain, swelling, warmth, red/erythema – usually left leg (left iliac vein compression by gravid uterus). Homan's sign (unreliable).
- **PE:** Dyspnoea (most common), pleuritic chest pain, tachypnoea, tachycardia, cough, haemoptysis. Syncope or hypotension in massive PE.
- **Upper limb DVT:** rare – arm swelling, pain, collateral veins (consider central line-related).
- **Postpartum:** Days 1–42 after delivery; peak risk in first week.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **DVT:**
  - **Superficial thrombophlebitis** – tender, palpable cord, no distal swelling.
  - **Cellulitis** – erythema with fever, lymphangitis, no oedema.
  - **Muscle haematoma/r/o** – bruising, trauma history, unilateral haematocrit drop.
  - **Baker's cyst** – usually popliteal fossa, not extending to calf.
  - **Physiological oedema** – bilateral, mild, resolves overnight.
- **PE:**
  - **Pneumothorax** – sudden unilateral chest pain, hyperresonance, tracheal shift if tension.
  - **Pneumonia** – fever, purulent sputum, focal crackles.
  - **Asthma exacerbation** – wheeze, known history, reversible.
  - **Musculoskeletal chest pain** – reproducible tenderness.
  - **Anxiety/hyperventilation** – normal saturations, normal blood gases, triggers.
- **Splitter:** **Unilateral leg swelling + positive compression ultrasound (CUS) → DVT; bilateral leg swelling + proteinuria + hypertension → pre-eclampsia, not DVT.**

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** ECG (tachycardia, S1Q3T3 if massive PE; often normal), pulse oximetry (SaO2 <94% suspicious), D-dimer **NOT useful** – physiologically elevated in pregnancy (use clinical probability first).
- **Bloods:** FBC (platelets – baseline before anticoagulation), clotting screen (PT, APTT), renal function (LMWH dose adjustment), LFTs (if anticoagulation history), VBG/ABG (low PaO2, respiratory alkalosis).
- **Imaging:**
  - **For suspected DVT:** **Compression ultrasound (CUS)** of leg veins – first line. If negative but high clinical suspicion, repeat CUS in 1 week or consider whole-leg ultrasound.
  - **For suspected PE:**
    - **Chest X-ray** – done to look for alternate diagnosis (pneumothorax, pneumonia). If abnormal → CTPA. If normal → **V/Q scan** or CTPA.
    - **CTPA** – lower radiation to fetal breast but higher to maternal breast (lifetime risk). Use modern low-dose protocols.
    - **Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan** – higher fetal radiation, lower maternal breast. Acceptable alternative if CXR normal and no contraindications.
    - **Echocardiogram** – emergently if massive PE to assess right heart strain.
  - **Never-miss test:** **Compression ultrasound (for DVT)** or **CTPA/VQ (for PE)** – omitting imaging in a high-probability patient is lethal.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Modified Wells score for PE in pregnancy is not validated** – use **PERC rule** (if all negative, low risk) but in pregnancy with dyspnoea/tachycardia, imaging is usually needed. **For DVT,** clinical diagnosis is unreliable; ultrasound is gold standard.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If massive PE with shock:** Call senior + ICU; consider immediate thrombolysis (e.g. alteplase 0.6 mg/kg IV over 15 min – only after ruling out intracranial haemorrhage and absolute contraindications). Do **not** delay for CTPA if bedside echo shows RV strain and clinical suspicion is high.
  - **Raise legs** for DVT (not for PE – increase preload slowly).
  - **Oxygen** to target SpO2 >94%.
  - **IV access**, baseline bloods.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Diagnostic imaging** (see above).
  - **Anticoagulation:**
    - **Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH)** – first line (enoxaparin or dalteparin). **Do not use unfractionated heparin (UFH) unless massive PE or severe renal impairment.**
    - Dosing: based on booking weight (ideally early pregnancy weight, otherwise current weight). Give weight-adjusted once daily.
    - For acute VTE: treatment dose. Example: enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily or 1 mg/kg twice daily (both acceptable – check local policy). Dalteparin 200 U/kg once daily.
    - **Check platelets weekly for first 2 weeks** (HIT very rare with LMWH but document).
    - **Anti-Xa monitoring** – not routinely required except extremes of body weight (<50 kg or >90 kg) or renal impairment – [check local policy].
  - **Thrombolysis** – only for massive PE with haemodynamic instability; discuss with obstetric medicine + haematology + cardiothoracic.
  - **IVC filter** – rarely indicated; only if contraindication to anticoagulation (e.g. active bleeding, upcoming delivery within 2 weeks with new DVT) – [check local policy].
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue LMWH for remainder of pregnancy and at least 6 weeks postpartum (total minimum 3 months).
  - **Postpartum:** Switch to warfarin (INR 2–3) or continue LMWH – warfarin is safe postpartum, but LMWH preferred in many units if patient prefers no injections or has contraindications.
  - **Plan for delivery:** Convert to UFH (if using LMWH and regional anaesthesia planned) – stop LMWH at least 24h before induction/C-section. [Check local policy for exact timing; often 12–24h.]
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid only if hypotensive; avoid fluid overload in right heart strain.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not applicable once on treatment dose LMWH.
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM if thrombolysis or surgery planned.
  - **Observation:** 4-hourly vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2) for first 24h; continue daily weight (for LMWH dose recalculation if weight changes >10%).
  - **Mobilisation:** Active mobilisation (no bed rest) – compression stockings (if DVT, after acute swelling reduced).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line – LMWH:** Enoxaparin (Clexane) – **treatment dose**: 1.5 mg/kg once daily SC OR 1 mg/kg twice daily SC. Dalteparin (Fragmin) – 200 U/kg once daily SC. Dosing based on **booking weight** (or current if >20% difference).
- **Alternatives:** UFH – IV infusion (target APTT 1.5–2.5 baseline) or SC twice daily – used if severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min) or near delivery. Fondaparinux – if HIT (rare in pregnancy).
- **Pain control:** Paracetamol (1g QDS PO/IV); avoid NSAIDs (risk of premature ductus closure, oligohydramnios).
- **Key contraindications:** Active bleeding, recent haemorrhagic stroke, severe thrombocytopenia (<50×10⁹/L), epidural/spinal anaesthesia planned within 12–24h of LMWH dose.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** In severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min) – use UFH (dose with APTT monitoring) or reduce LMWH dose – [check local policy]. Hepatic impairment: no specific adjustment but monitor clotting.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily to a woman >100 kg without checking anti-Xa – risk of underdosing in obesity; consider twice-daily regimen or anti-Xa monitoring.** Also: **Not holding LMWH 24h before epidural/spinal** – risk of spinal haematoma.

### Complications
- **Acute:** **Massive PE** (death), **paradoxical embolism** (arterial occlusion), **heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)** – very rare with LMWH, **bleeding** (minor > major; risk of retroplacental haematoma, postpartum haemorrhage).
- **Chronic:** **Post-thrombotic syndrome** (leg swelling, pain, venous ulcers – up to 50% of DVT patients), **chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension** (CTEPH – post-PE, dyspnoea, right heart failure – rare).

### Prognosis
- Maternal mortality from VTE ~1–2% overall; higher with massive PE. Fetal loss rate ~5% in treated VTE. Full recovery likely with anticoagulation; post-thrombotic syndrome common but can be minimised with early compression stockings.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use a clinical probability tool before imaging** (e.g. simplified Geneva or PERC) – but in pregnancy, any clinical suspicion + hypoxia or unilateral leg swelling = imaging.
- **Check booking weight for LMWH dose** – avoid using current weight late in pregnancy if oedema distorts.
- **Prescribe compression stockings** for DVT (after oedema reduced) – reduce post-thrombotic syndrome.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use D-dimer to rule out VTE in pregnancy** – it is elevated physiologically and in many conditions; leads to unnecessary imaging or false negatives.
- **Do not start LMWH without checking baseline FBC (platelets) and coagulation** – to identify pre-existing coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia.
- **Do not continue LMWH through delivery** – must be timed or switched to UFH to allow regional anaesthesia and reduce bleeding risk.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Chest pain, shortness of breath, haemoptysis, leg swelling/worsening pain, bleeding from any site, vaginal bleeding, abrupt severe headache.
- **Follow-up:** Obstetric medicine clinic within 1 week; haematology (if anticoagulation complex); postpartum anticoagulation plan; repeat ultrasound at 6 weeks if DVT to check recanalisation.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Massive PE:** Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) + hypoxia → call ICU/medical emergency team (MET); consider thrombolysis.
- **Signs of bleeding:** Vaginal bleeding > any spotting, or retroplacental haematoma (abdominal pain + fetal distress) → call senior obstetrician; stop LMWH until assessed.
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or **lactate >2 mmol/L** – escalate to registrar/consultant.
- **GCS drop** or **seizure** – rule out cerebral venous thrombosis; immediate CT head/venogram.
